title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created

input_row.title: Ensure that a minimal audit policy is created
input_row.description: Kubernetes can audit the details of requests made to the API server. The --audit- policy-file flag must be set for this logging to be enabled.
input_row.rationale: Logging is an important detective control for all systems, to detect potential unauthorised access. Impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.
input_row.audit: Run the following command on one of the cluster master nodes: ps -ef | grep kube-apiserver Verify that the --audit-policy-file is set. Review the contents of the file specified and ensure that it contains a valid audit policy.
input_row.remediation: Create an audit policy file for your cluster. Default Value: Unless the --audit-policy-file flag is specified, no auditing will be carried out. References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/debug-application-cluster/audit/
input_row.impact: Audit logs will be created on the master nodes, which will consume disk space. Care should be taken to avoid generating too large volumes of log information as this could impact the available of the cluster nodes.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**PROGRAM NAME:** k8s_api_server_audit_policy_file_configured

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

- **Kubernetes API calls or kubectl SDK methods to use:**
  - Query the kube-apiserver Pod(s) or static Pod manifest(s) in the kube-system namespace.
  - Inspect the command-line arguments or container args for the presence of the --audit-policy-file flag.

- **Kubernetes CLI commands (if applicable):**
  - For clusters using static Pods (most kubeadm and self-hosted setups):
    - `kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}'`
    - Or, for more detail: `kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l component=kube-apiserver -o json`
    - Alternatively, inspect the static Pod manifest directly if accessible (e.g., `/etc/kubernetes/manifests/kube-apiserver.yaml`), but this typically requires host access.
  - For managed Kubernetes services, use the provider's API or configuration interface to check API server flags.

- **Specific resources to enumerate:**
  - All kube-apiserver Pods or static Pod manifests in the cluster.

- **Exact configuration properties/settings to validate:**
  - The kube-apiserver must be started with the `--audit-policy-file` flag set to a valid file path.
  - The referenced audit policy file should exist and be a valid Kubernetes audit policy (JSON or YAML).

- **Clear Pass/Fail criteria with expected values:**
  - **Pass:** All running kube-apiserver instances have the `--audit-policy-file` flag set with a non-empty value.
  - **Fail:** Any kube-apiserver instance is missing the `--audit-policy-file` flag or it is set to an empty value.

- **Example:**
  - Use `kubectl -n kube-system get pods -l component=kube-apiserver -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].command}'` to extract the command line and verify the presence of `--audit-policy-file=<path>`.
  - Optionally, validate that the file path exists in the container's filesystem (requires further automation or host access).

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES BENCHMARK V1.10 PDF
